description: If something exists, then something exists — no matter what you call it.
goal: (-> (exists x (App P x)) (exists y (App P y)))

guides.text: The goal: $\exists x.\; P(x) \to \exists y.\; P(y)$. Obviously true — if something is $P$, then something is $P$, no matter what variable name you use. But proving it requires `exists-elim`, the most complex first-order rule.

`exists-elim` works like this: you have an existential claim ('something exists'). You say 'let's call that thing $y$' (a fresh [witness|witness]), prove your conclusion using $y$, and then close the witness. The conclusion must not depend on which specific thing $y$ was — because you never knew its identity.
Assume the existential claim: something is $P$.
Introduce a fresh [witness|witness] $y$ with `fix` — 'let $y$ be some thing.'
Assume this witness satisfies $P$: $P(y)$. This is the body of the existential, instantiated under the fresh name $y$.
Merge the witness atom $y$ with $P(y)$ using `and-intro` so both are in the same context.
From $P(y)$, form $\exists y.\; P(y)$ using `exists-intro`. Replace the concrete term $y$ with the binding variable $y$.

Now `exists-elim` closes the witness. It takes the existential (step 1), the witness subproof (step 6), and the variable name $y$. The engine checks $y$ doesn't leak into the conclusion, then discharges both the witness atom and $P(y)$.
Discharge the existential assumption to finish.

unlocks.name: ∃-elim
unlocks.description: From an existential claim, open a witness $y$, prove the conclusion $\psi$ under the assumption $\varphi[y/x]$, and discharge — provided $y$ doesn't escape into $\Delta$ or $\psi$.
